titleStmt.title: Letter from John Dillon to John Redmond, 19 June 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: John Dillon

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
Letter from John Dillon (1851-1927) to John Redmond (1856-1918). Dillon writes to Redmond to discuss party issues, including a conversation he had with Joe Devlin MP (1871-1934) on the situation in Belfast.
John Redmond was an MP and leader of the Irish Parliamentary Party. He was instrumental in securing the promise of Home Rule from Asquith's liberal government. Upon the outbreak of war, Redmond addressed a group of Irish Volunteers at Woodenbridge, Co. Wicklow, encouraging them to fight with the British Army to ensure the implementation of Home Rule. Redmond's words split the Irish Volunteers across the country. The Easter Rising shocked Redmond, who condemned it bitterly. Nonetheless he appealed for leniency towards those who had not been involved in planning the Rising. John Dillon was an Irish Party M.P. in the House of Commons. Dillon issued a speech in the House on 11 May 1916 where he strongly condemned the British handling of the Rising which had been put down “with so much blood and so much savagery”, and called for an immediate end to executions. Dillon would succeed John Redmond as leader of the Home Rule party in 1918.

correspAction.placeName: 2 North Great George's Street, Dublin, Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
2, NORTH GT GEORGE'S STREET,
DUBLIN. Monday 19 June 1916 My dear Redmond I have just had a
long battle with Devlin over
the telephone. He give a
most cheering account
the situation in Belfast.
The account in the Independent
of the meeting - 5am
outrageous travesty. I was was
an immense success and
practically unanimous.
I expect you will have an
account for Hannah.
Devlin's very strongest
opinion that you & I
should go in to Belfast
on Wednesday evening —
or at latest by the early
train on Thursday morning.
He thinkks it will be of the
utmost importance to
be there to interview important
people during Thursday. Let me know what you
decide to do. D is making arrangments
to lessen the six
Counties. On the other hand
Megher writes that the
Irish are working
hard answering against
the proposal. A special messenger
brought me a letter from
George yesterday - telling me
that now all the
Unionist Ministers have gone
into revolt. Bonar Law's
attitude is not known as
he's away in Paris. I am strongly of opinion
that some money should
be spent in enabling
the Fiennan in
for the next few days. Fallon who had a
most successful meeting
of the South Monaghan
Executive. The longest
that had been held for
a long time - and quite
unanimous — tell me
that Murphy has
several agents distributing
the Independant in
Ulster. But for the abominable
printing. The Freeman
of Saturday and
today are quite good
campaign douments
I am taking some
steps on my own
responsibility to circulate
it — This will of
course cost something. I shall write tonight
or tomorrow morning
about the overdraft. Yours Sincerely John Dillon
